coredata.dc:title: Correlation Between Portal Vein Pressure and Subharmonic Scattering Signals From SonoVue Microbubbles in Canines
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc. on behalf of World Federation for Ultrasound in Medicine & Biology.

Xu, Gang
Wang, Yun
Yang, Huayu
Li, Deyu

Su, Min
Li, Changcan

Mao, Yilei
Yu, Wenkui
Li, Fei
coredata.dc:description: The current gold standard for the clinical diagnosis of portal hypertension (PH) is an invasive and indirect estimation of portal vein pressure (PVP). Therefore, the need for a non-invasive PVP measurement method is urgent. Subharmonic scattering of ultrasound contrast agent (UCA) microbubbles is under investigation in clinical research as a pressure indicator. However, the driving acoustic pressure must be optimized to improve the ambient pressure sensitivity of the subharmonic amplitude for different UCAs. In this study, for the first time, we obtained the relationship between the PVP and the amplitude of the subharmonic signal scattered from SonoVue microbubbles by using two canines to build the PH model. The results revealed a desirable linear correlation between the subharmonic amplitude and PVP (<20 mmHg) at the incident acoustic pressure of 453 kPa (r = −0.910, p < 0.005; sensitivity: −2.003 dB/mmHg); this was one order of magnitude higher in sensitivity than that of the in vitro case with a detectable pressure variation of approximately 1 mmHg. This indicates the feasibility of using UCA microbubbles to accurately measure low ambient pressures in vivo and further exhibits the potential of the method for non-invasive pressure estimation in clinical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Non-invasive pressure estimation
Portal vein pressure
Subharmonic scattering
Ambient pressure
Ultrasound contrast agent microbubbles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Circuit diagram and photograph of the transmit/receive (T/R) switch.

Single scattered signal from SonoVue microbubbles at 453 kPa peak-negative incident acoustic pressure under a portal vein pressure of 8.10 mmHg and the corresponding power spectra. (a) Pulse 1 was the time-domain signal of the received echo of the transmitted pulse with a 0° phase, and pulse 2 was the time-domain signal of the received echo of the transmitted pulse with a 180° phase. (b) Corresponding power spectra of the received signals.

Correlation between the subharmonic signals of SonoVue microbubbles and portal vein pressures (PVPs) at peak-negative acoustic pressures of 421 and 453 kPa. (a, d) Pulse 1 + pulse 2. (b, e) Pulse 1. (c, f) Pulse 2.
Correlations between PVP and mean subharmonic amplitude

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: PVP = portal vein pressure; RMSE = root mean square error.

head.ce:title: Correlation Between Portal Vein Pressure and Subharmonic Scattering Signals From SonoVue Microbubbles in Canines

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Huimin Lu, Gang Xu and Yun Wang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The current gold standard for the clinical diagnosis of portal hypertension (PH) is an invasive and indirect estimation of portal vein pressure (PVP). Therefore, the need for a non-invasive PVP measurement method is urgent. Subharmonic scattering of ultrasound contrast agent (UCA) microbubbles is under investigation in clinical research as a pressure indicator. However, the driving acoustic pressure must be optimized to improve the ambient pressure sensitivity of the subharmonic amplitude for different UCAs. In this study, for the first time, we obtained the relationship between the PVP and the amplitude of the subharmonic signal scattered from SonoVue microbubbles by using two canines to build the PH model. The results revealed a desirable linear correlation between the subharmonic amplitude and PVP (<20 mmHg) at the incident acoustic pressure of 453 kPa (r = −0.910, p < 0.005; sensitivity: −2.003 dB/mmHg); this was one order of magnitude higher in sensitivity than that of the in vitro case with a detectable pressure variation of approximately 1 mmHg. This indicates the feasibility of using UCA microbubbles to accurately measure low ambient pressures in vivo and further exhibits the potential of the method for non-invasive pressure estimation in clinical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Portal hypertension (PH) can cause complications including upper digestive bleeding, ascites, infection, hepatic encephalopathy and renal failure (Simonetto et al. 2019). Therefore, monitoring portal vein pressure (PVP) is of considerable prognostic and clinical significance. The hepatic venous pressure gradient (HVPG) has been the gold standard indicator for evaluating PH since 1951 (Myers and Taylor 1951; Garcia-Tsao et al. 2017); an HVPG value >5 mmHg is the clinical threshold for diagnosing PH. HVPG indirectly measures PVP through a catheter placed in the hepatic vein and, thus, only estimates PVP. In addition, it is an invasive process; thus, some complications remain possible, such as hematoma, bleeding, bile leakage and arteriovenous fistulas (Bosch et al. 2009). Therefore, there is an urgent requirement for the development of a non-invasive method that can accurately assess PVP to meet clinical diagnosis and treatment requirements.

Ultrasound contrast agent (UCA) microbubbles, which are widely used to improve imaging quality for clinical diagnosis, are a promising tool for the non-invasive measurement of blood pressure. These comprise free gas bubbles encapsulated by lipid or polymer shells that reduce the surface tension against dissolution (Calliada et al. 1998). Owing to the compressible inner gas, the microbubble changes in size with the ambient pressure. Consequently, the acoustic properties, including frequency of the microbubble resonance, echo amplitude, disappearance time and non-linear scattering, change accordingly (Li et al. 2012; Xu et al. 2019). Therefore, blood pressure can be obtained based on the principles of acoustic properties as a function of ambient pressure. Owing to the superior ambient pressure dependence of the subharmonic scattering component from the microbubble's non-linear oscillation, the technique of subharmonic assisted pressure estimation (SHAPE) has been proposed to evaluate the local ambient pressure around the microbubbles (Shi et al. 1999). This technique, based on the subharmonic amplitude as a function of ambient pressure, has been researched extensively (Forsberg et al. 2000; Andersen and Jensen 2010; Dave et al. 2017; Li et al. 2018; Esposito et al. 2019, 2020, 2021; Nio et al. 2020; Dockerill et al. 2021; Xu et al. 2021; Qiao et al. 2022). Over the years, several clinical studies have compared the SHAPE gradient between the portal and hepatic veins with HVPG measurements for diagnosing patients with PH (Eisenbrey et al. 2013; Gupta et al. 2021). According to a clinical study (125 cases) of the UCA Sonazoid, a limited correlation between the two measurements (r = 0.68) indicated that SHAPE can be used in clinical diagnosis; however, the pressure sensitivity requires further optimization to improve the accuracy of low overpressure measurement (Gupta et al. 2021). Thus, a primary goal for future clinical applications of SHAPE is the optimization of the acoustic pressure for different types of UCAs to obtain high sensitivity and correlation with PVPs (Dave et al. 2013; Gupta et al. 2017; Esposito et al. 2021).

Our previous in vitro experiments confirmed that the subharmonic amplitude-versus-acoustic pressure curve of SonoVue microbubbles comprises three stages: the first growth stage, saturation and the second growth stage. In the second growth stage, the subharmonic amplitudes attain a desirable sensitivity and correlation with ambient pressures, which may be suitable for clinical low-pressure measurements (<40 mmHg), such as PVP (Xu et al. 2021). To investigate the possibility of in vivo pressure measurement by SonoVue microbubbles and determine the optimal acoustic pressure parameters, we explored the in vivo relationship between the subharmonic amplitudes of SonoVue microbubbles and PVP in the second growth stage.

This study was approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of the Shenzhen Advanced Animal Study Service Center (Approval Reference No. AAS C200808D). On the basis of the guidelines summarized for the animal model of PH, two canines were chosen for this study (Abraldes et al. 2006). Before experiments, the canines were fasted 24 h to reduce the influence of food on PVP after meals. Following an intravenous injection of propofol (Abbot Labs, North Chicago, IL, USA; dose: 7 mL/kg), the two canines were placed on heated blankets to maintain their body temperature. The canines were intubated, and anesthesia was administered along with 0.5%–2% isoflurane (Isothesia, Abbott Laboratories, North Chicago, IL, USA) through the endotracheal tube. We placed the 18-gauge cannula in the forelimb veins of the canines for SonoVue microbubble infusion. Throughout the experiment, the canines were monitored by certified veterinary technicians.

The main portal vein was accessed through a midline abdominal incision. After SonoVue microbubbles were pumped into the circulatory system via the forelimb vein, the pulse–echo system with a single-element transducer was switched on to generate acoustic waves and receive acoustic scattering signals from the SonoVue microbubbles flowing through the focal area. After collection of the signals, a closed intravenous catheter system (REF 383057, Becton Dickinson Medical Devices Co., Ltd., Suzhou, Jiangsu, China) connected to a control unit (MP160, BIOPAC Systems, Inc., Goleta, CA, USA) was inserted into the main portal vein to obtain the reference pressure. A gelatin sponge particle embolization agent (350–560 μm, ALICON Pharm SCI&TEC Co., Ltd., Hangzhou, Zhejiang, China) was used to induce a slow-flow (increased resistance) model of PH by embolizing the liver microcirculation. One of the canines was injected twice to increase the pressure. Finally, the canines were sacrificed after the experiments.

As seen on the right side of Figure 1 , we upgraded the acoustic scattering measurement system of our previous in vitro experiments (Xu et al. 2021) into a pulse inversion system with the pulse–echo mode, using only one transducer for transmitting and receiving acoustic waves for in vivo PVP measurements. Briefly, a programmable arbitrary waveform generator (DG4062, RIGOL Technologies Co., Ltd., Suzhou, Jiangsu, China) adopted dual channels with a delay of 150 µs to divide the phases into 0° and 180° to realize pulse inversion technology. The generated signals were further amplified using a broadband radio-frequency power amplifier (LZY-22+, Mini-Circuits, Brooklyn, NY, USA). The acoustic signals scattered by the SonoVue microbubbles were received by the same transducer and passed through a transmit/receive (T/R) switch (MD0100, Supertex Inc., Sunnyvale, CA, USA) as illustrated in Figure 2 , which transmitted and received acoustic waves using the same 3.5 MHz transducer with a focal length of 25 mm and a −6 dB bandwidth of 2.34 to 4.87 MHz (V306-SU, Panametrics, Waltham, MA, USA). This generated a 16-cycle tone burst with a center frequency of 4 MHz and further received the scattered signals from microbubbles. The received signals were further amplified using a pre-amplifier (Model 5800PR, Panametrics) and digitized at a sampling frequency of 100 MHz (Octopus 822F, Gage, Lockport, IL, USA). The peak negative acoustic pressures at the focus were calibrated with a three-dimensional acoustic field measurement system (BRC8090, Shenzhen Boray Technology Co. Ltd., Shenzhen, Guangdong, China) using a needle hydrophone (0.5 mm Needle Probe, Precision Acoustics, Dorchester, UK). In our previous study, we described a procedure for preparing a SonoVue microbubble suspension (Li et al. 2018). Briefly, after 5 mL of saline was injected into the septum-sealed vial with SF6 and 25 mg of lyophilized product, the vial was manually shaken for 20 s to prepare the native suspension with a concentration of 2 × 108 bubbles/mL measured by particle sizing systems (AccuSizer 780, Particle Sizing Systems, Inc., Santa Barbara, CA, USA). The concentration used in our experiment was converted according to the number of microbubbles in the canine pressure measurement experiment for Sonazoid (Dave et al., 2012b). In the experiment conducted by Dave et al., (2012b), Sonazoid with a concentration of 2–5 × 108 bubbles/mL was infused at the rate of 0.015 mL/kg/min, and the mean weight of canines was 22.5 ± 1.45 kg. In our study, the mean weight of the two canines was 11.25 kg; blood volume is generally considered to be proportional to body weight. The native SonoVue suspension was diluted 10 times with saline and continuously pumped at the flow rate of 0.33 mL/min through the forelimb veins of the canines; therefore, the number of microbubbles was approximately equal to that used by Dave et al. (2012b). During the current experiment, the transducer was sheathed in an additively manufactured waveguide tube that was filled with saline. The edge of the waveguide tube was covered with a tight film, which was closely attached to the wall of the portal vein. The diameters of the main portal veins of the canines were measured with a commercial ultrasonic scanner (Resona 7, Mindray Medical Inc., Shenzhen, Guangdong, China). The tube length plus the vessel radius was designed to ensure that the focus of the transducer was close to the center of the portal vein. Then, the transducer was held by a portable stereotaxic apparatus, which was further manually tuned to match the distances d 1 and d 2 from top and bottom vessel walls to the transducer surface and the focal length f to the relationship f = (d 1 + d 2)/2, according to the corresponding time of flight of the pulse–echo signal. Each acoustic pressure and PVP was measured in three sets comprising five groups of data.

We used MATLAB 7.0 (The MathWorks Inc., Natick, MA, USA) to post-process the pulse data received. The fast Fourier transform was used to further calculate the average power spectrum of the 500 received signals, and each signal was apodized through the Hanning window (Fig. 3 ). The subharmonic amplitude was determined as the peak subharmonic amplitude of the averaged spectrum.

The average subharmonic amplitude of the three sets of measurements was expressed as the mean value ± standard deviation. Analyses were performed using IBM SPSS Statistics (Version 23.0, IBM Corp., Armonk, NY, USA). Pearson correlation analyses were performed to identify the relationship between the PVPs and subharmonic signal amplitudes at different acoustic pressures, as well as between the PVPs and mean subharmonic signal amplitudes. Furthermore, the deviation between the estimated pressure predicted by the subharmonic amplitude and the reference standard (the pressure catheter) was determined by the root mean square error (RMSE). Statistical significance was set at p < 0.05.

The time-domain signals and power spectra of the received echoes are illustrated in Figure 3. Before pulse inversion, signals from the blood vessel walls can be clearly observed in pulses 1 and 2, and the SonoVue microbubble scattering signals cannot be recognized in Figure 3a. Although the subharmonic frequency around 2 MHz was slightly beyond the −6 dB bandwidth of the commercial transducer, an obvious subharmonic peak remained visible in the spectrum of the received signal, as illustrated in Figure 3b. In the future, a customized transducer with a –6 dB bandwidth spanning the frequency range 2–4 MHz will be considered to further improve the transducer's sensitivity to the subharmonic component. After pulse inversion, the 0° and 180° phase pulses were superimposed, and the signals of the blood vessel walls and other interference signals were canceled, leaving only the obvious SonoVue microbubble scattering signals. In addition, from the power spectrum of the received echo, the pulse inversion method suppressed the fundamental components and enhanced the subharmonic amplitude. The pulse inversion technique will help to raise the signal-to-noise ratio of scattering signals around 2 MHz.

Our previous in vitro study found that the subharmonic scattering power of SonoVue microbubbles has two growth stages, where in the second growth stage (400–540 kPa), the subharmonic amplitudes attained a better linear relationship with the ambient pressures, as well as desirable sensitivities; therefore, this stage was more suitable than the first stage (40–300 kPa) for estimating low ambient pressures (Xu et al. 2021). Additionally, in this animal experiment, we selected the acoustic pressure of the second growth stage for in vivo experimental verification. However, the circuit load of the current pulse inversion system with dual transmitting channels was twice that of our previous experimental setup consisting of one transducer as a transmitter and the other as a receiver. The excitation voltage of the pulse inversion system was therefore twice that of the previous system to generate the same acoustic pressure at the focus, which may be high enough to damage the T/R switch, pre-amplifier and signal acquisition card. Therefore, the maximum acoustic pressure obtained by the pulse-reversal system was limited to 453 kPa. Consequently, we chose two acoustic pressure values of 421 and 453 kPa in the second growth stage, which used safe voltages for our system to explore the in vivo correlation between PVP and subharmonic signal amplitude.

The weights and PVPs of the two canines are outlined in Table 1 . The change in PVP in this experiment (7.50–15.09 mmHg) was smaller than the pressure increment in our previous in vitro experiment (10–40 mmHg with a 10 mmHg increment) (Xu et al. 2021). Under each acoustic pressure, all 15 subharmonic amplitudes of the three sets corresponding to each PVP are plotted for pulse 1 + pulse 2 (pulse inversion) and for pulse 1 and pulse 2 separately, as illustrated in Figure 4 . At acoustic pressures of 421 and 453 kPa, for the cases of pulse 1 + pulse 2 in Figure 4 (a, d), subharmonic amplitudes attained a desirable linear relationship with the PVPs (r = −0.861, p < 0.005, and r = −0.910, p < 0.005, respectively) and high sensitivities (−1.842 and −2.003 dB/mmHg, respectively). For pulse 1 (r = –0.801, sensitivity: –1.106 dB/mmHg; r = −0.920, sensitivity: −1.233 dB/mmHg) in Figure 4 (b, e) and pulse 2 (r = −0.612, sensitivity: −0.908 dB/mmHg; r = −0.921, sensitivity: −1.251 dB/mmHg) in Figure 4 (c, f), the liner correlation coefficients were comparable to those of pulse 1 + pulse 2, while both pulse 1 and pulse 2 had much lower sensitivities compared with the combined pulse 1 + pulse 2. This was attributed to the subharmonic enhancement caused by the pulse inversion method for exciting the microbubbles’ non-linear oscillations and the post-processing of echo signals.

Although the linear correlation was lower than the corresponding acoustic pressures in our previous in vitro experiments (Xu et al. 2021), the sensitivities under these two acoustic pressures were significantly higher than our previous results and those reported by Nio et al. (maximum sensitivity: 0.16 dB/mmHg) (Nio et al. 2020; Xu et al. 2021). The in vivo results in this study had a sensitivity one order of magnitude higher than those of the in vitro experiments. In addition, for the case of pulse 1 + pulse 2, all the data points of the subharmonic amplitudes were completely separated between PVPs of 14.20 and 15.09 mmHg at acoustic pressures of 421 and 453 kPa in Figure 4 (a, d). Consequently, a pressure change smaller than 1 mmHg could be distinguished between PVPs of 14.20 and 15.09 mmHg at both 421 and 453 kPa. This may be explained by the significant effect of body temperature on the shell structure, stability and dynamic behavior of SonoVue microbubbles (Guiot et al. 2006; Vos et al. 2008; Mulvana et al. 2011; Sun et al. 2016). Under the same acoustic parameters and ambient pressure, a majority of the microbubbles at body temperature were destroyed in contrast to the bubbles at room temperature (25°C in our previous in vitro experiment) (Mulvana et al. 2011), resulting in a marked pressure sensitivity of the subharmonic amplitude. A series of studies on Sonazoid microbubbles also revealed that the pressure sensitivity of the subharmonic amplitude in in vivo experiments was higher than that in in vitro experiments (Dave et al. 2013), which showed an in vitro sensitivity range of −0.22 to −0.07 dB/mmHg (Dave et al. 2011; Halldorsdottir et al. 2011) and an in vivo range of −0.96 to −0.20 dB/mmHg (Forsberg et al. 2005; Dave et al. 2012a, 2012c, 2012d).

It seems that, for current in vivo study, there exists an upper threshold of ambient pressure above which the subharmonic amplitude of SonoVue falls below the noise floor and cannot be used to estimate pressure. If the ambient pressure were increased to 20 mmHg, the subharmonic amplitude should have decreased to a very low level of about –81 dB, which would be difficult to detect according to the sensitivity under the acoustic pressure of 453 kPa, as illustrated in Figure 4d. This may be caused by several factors: (i) greater bubble destruction at body temperature resulted in acoustic scattering signals weaker than those at room temperature; (ii) the single-element transducer had a fixed and small focal region in which the number of microbubbles was insufficient to generate an obvious subharmonic scattering intensity under a high ambient pressure; (iii) because the current pulse inversion system had a limited working voltage range, the subharmonic intensity could not be further improved by increasing the acoustic pressure above the maximum excitation voltage for a high ambient pressure. Therefore, a higher ambient pressure may be estimated by introducing a scanning beam via a commercial ultrasonic scanner with a wide acoustic pressure range.

As outlined in Table 2 , under both acoustic pressures, the mean subharmonic amplitude decreases as the PVP increases, which is consistent with the results of our previous in vitro experiments: under a high level of acoustic pressure, the subharmonic amplitude decreases with increasing ambient pressure (Li et al. 2018; Xu et al. 2021). Furthermore, the acoustic pressure increases and the linear correlation coefficient and pressure sensitivity increase, respectively, which further matches our in vitro experiment (Xu et al. 2021). At the incident acoustic pressures of 421 and 453 kPa, the corresponding RMSEs were respectively 1.78 and 1.39 mmHg, which were lower than the RMSE in our in vitro experiment at the acoustic pressure of 480 kPa (Xu et al. 2021) because of the higher subharmonic sensitivity to ambient pressure in the current study. It was reported that for the subharmonic amplitude to successfully detect PH, the maximum error in estimating baseline PVPs was less than 2 mmHg; thus, SHAPE was a viable clinical tool for measuring PVPs (Dave et al., 2012b). The RMSE in our research was also less than 2 mmHg, indicating that the current in vivo results met the clinically acceptable error for accurately measuring PVP.

In this investigation, a single-element transducer-based pulse-echo system was developed to perform the pulse inversion technique to explore the in vivo correlation between the subharmonic signals from SonoVue microbubbles and PVP. Our study indicated that a high acoustic pressure during the second growth stage of the subharmonic amplitude-versus-acoustic pressure curve of SonoVue microbubbles may be suitable for accurate pressure measurement in vivo. However, the animal model of PH requires further improvement and acquisition of a wider range of blood pressure values to fully verify its feasibility. To completely translate the acoustic parameters in our previous in vitro study to a commercial transducer, more acoustic pressures should be tested to find the most suitable one for pressure measurement. In the future, commercial ultrasound scanners will be considered for pressure measurements to achieve image-guided pressure estimation for clinical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 81927808, 12074402, 12004411 and 81972698), the Shenzhen Basic Research Program (Nos. JCYJ20200109114825064 and JCYJ20170817171836611), the Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Ultrasound Imaging and Therapy (No. ZDSYS201802061806314), Shenzhen Science and Technology Program (No. GJHZ20210705141404012), the Key Laboratory for Magnetic Resonance and Multimodality Imaging of Guangdong Province (No. 2020B1212060051), the Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province (No. 2022NSFSC0843) and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2022M712262).

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare they have no potential conflicts of interest with respect to this article.
